card.intuition: A common database performance anti-pattern where an application makes one query to get a list of items, and then one additional query PER item to fetch related data.
card.analogy: Grocery shopping: instead of buying 10 items in one trip (1 query), you drive to the store for a bag of chips (1), drive home, then drive back for a soda (1), drive home... doing 11 trips total for 10 items.
when_to_use: Detecting why a page with a simple list is taking 10 seconds to load
Optimization phase of any application using an ORM (Active Record/Data Mapper)
gotchas: ORMs often 'Lazy Load' related data by default, which creates the N+1 problem unless you explicitly 'Eager Load' (using JOINs)
It is easy to miss in development with small datasets, but it explodes in production with thousands of records

exercise.prompt: Complete the ORM-style code to fix the N+1 problem using eager loading.
exercise.starter_code:
# BAD: Triggers N+1 queries
users = db.query(User).all()
for u in users:
    print(u.profile.bio)  # Fires 1 query per user!

# GOOD: Loads everything in 1 or 2 queries total
users = db.query(User).__________ (User.profile).all()
hints: In SQLAlchemy/ActiveRecord, you often use 'include' or 'joinedload' or 'prefetch'
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() in ["joinedload", "include", "prefetch_related"]
assert check("joinedload")
print("PASS")
